notes.title: Solutions — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Concentration, Raoult's law, and colligative properties of solutions.

## 1. Concentration Terms
- Molarity (mol/L), molality (mol/kg), mole fraction, mass %, ppm.
- Molality is preferred for colligative properties (temperature independent).

## 2. Solubility & Henry's Law
- Gas solubility ∝ pressure: p = K_H·x. Higher K_H → lower solubility.

## 3. Raoult's Law
- For volatile components: p = p°·x. Total pressure = p_A°x_A + p_B°x_B.
- **Ideal solutions** obey Raoult's law (ΔH_mix = 0, ΔV_mix = 0).
- **Positive deviation** (weaker A–B forces, e.g. ethanol+acetone); **negative deviation** (stronger, e.g. HNO₃+water).

## 4. Colligative Properties
- Depend on NUMBER of particles, not nature.
- Relative lowering of vapour pressure, **boiling point elevation** ΔTb = Kb·m, **freezing point depression** ΔTf = Kf·m, **osmotic pressure** π = CRT.
- **van't Hoff factor i:** accounts for dissociation/association (i>1 dissociation, i<1 association).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Colligative properties depend on particle COUNT — electrolytes give more particles (use i).
- Azeotropes show maximum/minimum boiling and cannot be separated by distillation.
- Osmotic pressure is best for macromolecules (measurable at low conc).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- p = p°x (Raoult); π = CRT.
- ΔTb = Kb·m, ΔTf = Kf·m.
- Use i for electrolytes.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For molar mass from colligative data, include the van't Hoff factor i when the solute dissociates.

notes.title: Electrochemistry — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Redox in cells: electrode potential, Nernst equation, conductance and electrolysis.

## 1. Galvanic (Voltaic) Cell
- Converts chemical energy to electrical. Anode (oxidation, −), cathode (reduction, +).
- Cell EMF E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode. Positive E°cell → spontaneous.

## 2. Electrode Potential & Nernst Equation
- Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) = 0 V reference.
- **Nernst:** E = E° − (0.059/n) log Q (at 298 K).
- At equilibrium E = 0, and E° = (0.059/n) log K.

## 3. Thermodynamics
- ΔG° = −nFE°cell (F = 96500 C/mol).

## 4. Conductance
- Conductivity κ, molar conductivity Λm = κ×1000/M.
- Λm increases on dilution; for strong electrolytes Λm = Λ°m − A√C (Debye–Hückel–Onsager). Weak electrolytes: Kohlrausch's law for Λ°m.

## 5. Electrolysis & Batteries
- **Faraday's laws:** mass deposited ∝ charge (m = ZIt); 1 mole electrons = 1 F = 96500 C.
- Primary (dry cell), secondary (lead storage, Ni-Cd — rechargeable), fuel cells.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- In galvanic cells anode is negative; in electrolytic cells anode is positive.
- Molar conductivity rises on dilution; conductivity falls.
- More positive E° → stronger oxidising agent.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode.
- Nernst: E = E° − (0.059/n)logQ.
- ΔG° = −nFE°; m = ZIt.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Remember 0.059/n for Nernst at 298 K and that a positive E°cell means a spontaneous reaction.

notes.title: Chemical Kinetics — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Reaction rates, order, rate laws and the Arrhenius equation.

## 1. Rate of Reaction
- Rate = change in concentration/time. Average vs instantaneous rate.
- Factors: concentration, temperature, catalyst, surface area.

## 2. Order & Molecularity
- **Order:** sum of powers of concentration in the rate law (experimental, can be fractional/zero).
- **Molecularity:** number of species in an elementary step (integer, theoretical).

## 3. Integrated Rate Laws
- **Zero order:** [A] = [A]₀ − kt; t₁/₂ = [A]₀/2k.
- **First order:** k = (2.303/t) log([A]₀/[A]); t₁/₂ = 0.693/k (independent of concentration).

## 4. Temperature Dependence
- **Arrhenius:** k = A·e^{−Ea/RT}; log(k₂/k₁) = (Ea/2.303R)(1/T₁ − 1/T₂).
- Rate roughly doubles for every 10°C rise.
- **Activation energy Ea:** minimum energy for reaction; catalyst lowers Ea.

## 5. Collision Theory
- Effective collisions need sufficient energy AND proper orientation.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Order is experimental; molecularity is theoretical (for elementary steps only).
- First-order half-life is independent of initial concentration.
- A catalyst lowers Ea for both forward and backward reactions.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- First order: t₁/₂ = 0.693/k.
- Arrhenius: k = A e^{−Ea/RT}.
- Catalyst lowers Ea.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
For radioactive-like first-order problems, use t₁/₂ = 0.693/k and the log form of the rate equation.

notes.title: d- and f-Block Elements — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Transition metals and inner transition metals — properties and trends.

## 1. General Characteristics (d-block)
- Partially filled d-orbitals. Show **variable oxidation states**, form coloured ions, paramagnetism, and good catalysts.
- Form complexes and alloys.

## 2. Trends
- Atomic radii decrease then become almost constant (poorly shielded d-electrons).
- **Colour:** due to d–d transitions. Colourless when d⁰ or d¹⁰ (Sc³⁺, Zn²⁺).
- **Magnetic moment** μ = √(n(n+2)) BM (n = unpaired electrons).

## 3. Important Compounds
- KMnO₄ (strong oxidiser, Mn +7) and K₂Cr₂O₇ (Cr +6) — preparation & oxidising action.

## 4. f-Block (Lanthanoids & Actinoids)
- **Lanthanoid contraction:** steady decrease in size → similar properties of 4d and 5d elements.
- Actinoids are radioactive, show more oxidation states.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Zn, Cd, Hg are not regarded as typical transition metals (d¹⁰, no partially filled d in common states).
- Colour requires unpaired d-electrons (d–d transition).
- Lanthanoid contraction explains similar Zr/Hf radii.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Variable oxidation states, coloured ions, catalysis.
- μ = √(n(n+2)) BM.
- Lanthanoid contraction.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Use μ = √(n(n+2)) to find the number of unpaired electrons from a given magnetic moment.

notes.title: Coordination Compounds — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Complexes: nomenclature, bonding theories, isomerism and applications.

## 1. Terminology
- Central metal, ligands, coordination number, oxidation state.
- Ligand types: monodentate, bidentate (en), polydentate (EDTA), ambidentate (NO₂⁻/SCN⁻).

## 2. Werner's Theory & Nomenclature
- Primary (ionisable) and secondary (coordinate) valencies.
- IUPAC: name ligands alphabetically, then metal with oxidation state in Roman numerals.

## 3. Bonding — VBT & CFT
- **VBT:** hybridisation predicts geometry (sp³ tetrahedral, dsp² square planar, d²sp³/sp³d² octahedral).
- **Crystal Field Theory:** d-orbitals split (Δ). Strong field ligands (CO, CN⁻) → low spin; weak field (I⁻, Br⁻) → high spin.
- Spectrochemical series ranks ligand field strength.

## 4. Isomerism & Colour
- Structural (ionisation, linkage, coordination, hydrate) and stereoisomerism (geometrical, optical).
- Colour arises from d–d transitions; magnitude of Δ decides colour.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Strong field ligands cause pairing (low spin, small μ).
- EDTA is hexadentate; en is bidentate.
- Oxidation state ≠ coordination number.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- CFT: Δ splitting, strong vs weak field ligands.
- Spectrochemical series.
- Isomerism types.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
To decide high vs low spin, locate the ligand in the spectrochemical series (strong → low spin).

notes.title: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Alkyl/aryl halides: preparation, substitution/elimination mechanisms and reactivity.

## 1. Nature & Preparation
- C–X bond polar. Prepared from alcohols (with HX, PCl₅, SOCl₂), alkenes (HX addition), halogenation.

## 2. Nucleophilic Substitution
- **SN1:** two steps via carbocation, favoured by 3° halides, polar protic solvents; racemisation.
- **SN2:** one step, backside attack, favoured by 1° halides, polar aprotic solvents; inversion (Walden).
- Reactivity SN1: 3° > 2° > 1°; SN2: 1° > 2° > 3°.

## 3. Elimination
- E1/E2 give alkenes; **Saytzeff's rule** (more substituted alkene is major product).

## 4. Haloarenes
- C–X bond strong (resonance, partial double bond character) → less reactive to nucleophilic substitution.
- Electrophilic substitution: halogens are o/p-directing but deactivating.

## 5. Key Reactions & Effects
- Wurtz reaction, reaction with Mg (Grignard reagent).
- Optical activity and chirality of halides.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- SN1 favours 3°; SN2 favours 1° (opposite trends).
- Haloarenes are much less reactive than haloalkanes (resonance).
- SN2 causes inversion; SN1 causes racemisation.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- SN1 (carbocation, racemisation, 3°); SN2 (backside, inversion, 1°).
- Saytzeff for elimination.
- Haloarenes are unreactive to SN.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Match substrate + solvent to mechanism: 3° + protic → SN1; 1° + aprotic → SN2.

notes.title: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Oxygen-containing functional groups: preparation, properties and reactions.

## 1. Alcohols
- Preparation: hydration of alkenes, reduction of carbonyls, from Grignard reagents.
- Reactions: with HX, esterification, oxidation (1° → aldehyde → acid; 2° → ketone; 3° resistant), dehydration to alkenes.
- Acidic strength: water > 1° > 2° > 3° alcohol.

## 2. Phenols
- More acidic than alcohols (resonance stabilised phenoxide).
- Acidity increases with electron-withdrawing groups (−NO₂ at o/p).
- Reactions: electrophilic substitution (highly activated ring), Reimer–Tiemann, Kolbe, coupling.

## 3. Ethers
- Preparation: Williamson synthesis (alkoxide + alkyl halide).
- Cleavage by HI; relatively inert otherwise.

## 4. Tests
- Lucas test (distinguishes 1°, 2°, 3° alcohols), Victor Meyer test, ferric chloride test (phenols give violet colour).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Phenols are more acidic than alcohols but less than carboxylic acids.
- 3° alcohols react fastest with Lucas reagent (immediate turbidity).
- Williamson synthesis works best with primary alkyl halides (avoids elimination).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Acidity: carboxylic acid > phenol > water > alcohol.
- Williamson synthesis for ethers.
- Lucas test for alcohol classification.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Electron-withdrawing groups (−NO₂) at ortho/para increase phenol acidity — a common comparison MCQ.

notes.title: Aldehydes, Ketones & Carboxylic Acids — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Carbonyl compounds and carboxylic acids: nucleophilic addition and key named reactions.

## 1. Carbonyl Group (>C=O)
- Polar; undergoes **nucleophilic addition** (aldehydes more reactive than ketones — steric + electronic).
- Addition of HCN, NaHSO₃, alcohols (acetals), Grignard reagents.

## 2. Reactions of Aldehydes/Ketones
- **Reduction:** to alcohols (LiAlH₄/NaBH₄); to alkanes (Clemmensen, Wolff–Kishner).
- **Oxidation:** aldehydes oxidise easily (ketones resist).
- **Aldol condensation** (α-H needed), **Cannizzaro** (no α-H), **Haloform reaction** (methyl ketones).

## 3. Tests
- Tollens' (silver mirror) and Fehling's (red ppt) — positive for aldehydes, not ketones.
- Iodoform test for methyl ketones / CH₃CH(OH)−.

## 4. Carboxylic Acids
- Acidic (resonance-stabilised carboxylate). Acidity increases with electron-withdrawing groups (e.g. Cl).
- Reactions: form esters, acid chlorides, amides, anhydrides; decarboxylation; HVZ reaction.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Aldehydes give positive Tollens'/Fehling's; ketones do not.
- Cannizzaro needs NO α-H; aldol needs α-H.
- Electron-withdrawing groups increase acid strength.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Nucleophilic addition; aldehyde > ketone reactivity.
- Aldol (α-H) vs Cannizzaro (no α-H).
- Tollens'/Fehling's for aldehydes.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Use Tollens'/Fehling's to distinguish aldehydes from ketones; iodoform for methyl ketones.

notes.title: Amines — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Nitrogen-containing organics: classification, basicity and reactions including diazonium salts.

## 1. Classification & Preparation
- 1°, 2°, 3° amines and quaternary salts.
- Preparation: reduction of nitro compounds/nitriles, Gabriel synthesis (pure 1° amines), Hofmann bromamide (one carbon less).

## 2. Basicity
- Lone pair on N accepts protons. In aqueous phase: 2° > 1° > 3° (solvation + steric) for aliphatic amines.
- Aromatic amines (aniline) are weaker bases (lone pair delocalised into ring).

## 3. Reactions
- Acylation, alkylation, carbylamine reaction (1° amines, foul smell — test), Hinsberg test (distinguishes 1°,2°,3°).
- Reaction with HNO₂: 1° aliphatic → alcohol; 1° aromatic → diazonium salt (at 0–5°C).

## 4. Diazonium Salts
- Very useful: convert to halides (Sandmeyer), phenol, arenes; coupling reactions give azo dyes.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Aliphatic amine basicity order in water: 2° > 1° > 3° (not a simple trend).
- Aniline is less basic than ammonia (resonance).
- Carbylamine and Hinsberg tests distinguish amine classes.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Basicity: aliphatic > aromatic; 2°>1°>3° in water.
- Gabriel (pure 1°), Hofmann (one C less).
- Diazonium salts → many products.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Diazonium chemistry is a favourite — learn Sandmeyer and coupling reactions for guaranteed marks.

notes.title: Biomolecules — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Carbohydrates, proteins, nucleic acids, vitamins and hormones — high yield for NEET.

## 1. Carbohydrates
- Monosaccharides (glucose, fructose), disaccharides (sucrose — non-reducing; maltose, lactose — reducing), polysaccharides (starch, cellulose, glycogen).
- Glucose: aldohexose; reactions confirm structure. Reducing sugars give Tollens'/Fehling's test.

## 2. Proteins
- Made of α-amino acids (zwitterion form; isoelectric point).
- Peptide bond (–CO–NH–). Structure: primary, secondary (α-helix, β-sheet), tertiary, quaternary.
- **Denaturation:** loss of structure (heat/acid) — secondary & tertiary destroyed.

## 3. Enzymes
- Biological catalysts (proteins), highly specific, lower activation energy.

## 4. Nucleic Acids
- DNA & RNA: nucleotides (base + sugar + phosphate). DNA double helix; bases A–T, G–C (RNA: U replaces T).
- DNA stores genetic info; RNA in protein synthesis.

## 5. Vitamins & Hormones
- Fat-soluble (A, D, E, K) and water-soluble (B, C). Deficiency diseases.
- Hormones: chemical messengers (insulin, adrenaline, etc.).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Sucrose is non-reducing; glucose, fructose, maltose, lactose are reducing.
- Denaturation destroys 2°/3° structure but not primary (peptide bonds intact).
- Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) is water soluble; A, D, E, K are fat soluble.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Reducing vs non-reducing sugars.
- Protein structure levels; denaturation.
- DNA base pairing A–T, G–C.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Memorise which sugars are reducing/non-reducing and the fat- vs water-soluble vitamins — direct one-mark NEET questions.
